data.user_message:
Hello! I'm glad you reached out. Let me introduce myself — I'm the Director of Employment Services at the Palestinian Ministry of Labor. My division is responsible for connecting young Palestinians, mostly between fifteen and twenty-nine, with real work opportunities across the West Bank and Gaza. We have regional offices, a small central team, and we work with partners like the Palestinian Employment Fund and Enabel. Honestly, the thing that keeps me up at night is that young people come to us, register, we try to help them — and then they just... disappear. Six months later I have no idea if they found work, if their skills ever matched anything real. We're supposed to be making a difference, but I have nothing to show for it.

Right, so — what I'm hoping we can build is basically a central platform, a single place where young Palestinians looking for work can find real job opportunities, and where employers can actually find the right people without being flooded with unsuitable applicants. Right now everything is completely scattered — jobs get posted on different websites, passed around by word of mouth, written on paper notices — and my staff are spending all their time doing things by hand that should just... happen automatically. The people who would use this are young job seekers, employers of all kinds, my ministry staff, and honestly our regional office workers too. The main thing I want — and I cannot stress this enough — is that we stop losing track of people. A young person registers with us, we help them, and then we never know what happened. That has to change.
data.assistant_message: I understand the challenges you're facing. What are the three most important things a user must be able to do with this central platform?

data.user_message: Honestly, when you put it that way... the first thing — and this is the one that really matters to me — is that a young person can come to the platform, put in their information, their education, their skills, whatever experience they have, and then actually get connected to jobs that suit them. Not just any jobs — the right ones. The second thing is that employers need to be able to post their vacancies somewhere that my ministry can actually see, so we're not invisible to them anymore. And the third... I guess it's that my team and I can finally see what's happening — who registered, who got hired, which sectors are actually creating jobs. Because right now I cannot answer that question, and my government and international partners keep asking me for numbers I simply don't have.
data.assistant_message: I see, those are crucial aspects. How do you currently gather and share job listings with people looking for work in your community?

data.user_message: It's honestly a mess. Employers do whatever they want — some post on local job websites like jobs.ps, some put up paper notices, some just call people they know personally. When a young person walks into one of our offices, my staff sit with them, look at their paper certificates, maybe help them write a basic CV in Word, and then suggest a few employers they happen to know about from memory. There's no system, no central list — it's entirely based on who your staff member knows that day. And the employers who are actually hiring? They go straight to commercial websites and never think to contact the Ministry at all, so we have no visibility into what's out there. It's like we're operating blind.
data.assistant_message: It sounds like centralizing job listings would be beneficial. Can you tell me how long you keep job listings on your platform and if you need to create any reports about them, like how many jobs were posted in a month?

data.user_message: Oh, the reports question — that's actually a big one for me. My analysts are constantly being asked by the government and by our international partners like Enabel to produce labour market reports — which sectors are hiring, what skills are in demand, where the jobs are geographically. And right now we literally cannot answer those questions reliably. We're guessing. That's embarrassing and it undermines everything we're trying to do policy-wise. As for how long to keep listings... honestly I haven't thought much about that, I'd probably just want them visible as long as the job is open and then archived somewhere so we still have the data for our statistics.
data.assistant_message: Got it. How do you determine when a job is no longer open, and what should happen if an employer forgets to update the status?

data.user_message: That's a fair question — I guess the employer would need to mark it as filled or closed themselves, but honestly I don't trust that they'll always remember to do that. Maybe there could be some kind of reminder sent to them? I'm not sure how that would work exactly. But I don't want old job listings sitting there confusing young people who apply and then hear nothing back — that's already a problem we have and it's really discouraging for them.
data.assistant_message: Understood. How often should these reminders be sent to employers to ensure job listings are up-to-date?

data.user_message: I mean, I haven't really thought about the exact timing — maybe once a week if the job has been open for a while? But honestly this feels like a detail we can figure out later. What worries me more is the bigger picture — right now we have no connection at all with employers, so even getting them onto the platform in the first place feels like the harder problem. How do we make sure real, legitimate employers are the ones registering and posting jobs? I'd hate for the whole thing to be undermined by fake listings.
data.assistant_message: Ensuring legitimate employers register is important. What process do you envision for verifying employers before they can post jobs on the platform?

data.user_message: Honestly, I'm not entirely sure what the right process looks like — that's a bit outside what I normally deal with. But I'd want some basic check, like maybe they provide their business registration number or some official document that proves they're a real company operating legally. My staff could review those before approving them. What worries me a little is the workload — if hundreds of employers try to register, my team is small and I don't want this to become another thing drowning them in paperwork. Is there a way to make that verification process manageable without needing someone to manually check every single one?
data.assistant_message: I understand the concern about workload. What should happen if an employer's registration is flagged as potentially problematic — should it be automatically rejected, or should there be a manual review process?

data.user_message: I think there should always be a human checking in those cases — I wouldn't want someone automatically rejected if there's just a small mistake in their paperwork or something. But for straightforward cases where everything looks fine, maybe the system can just approve them without my staff needing to get involved every time. I guess what I'm saying is — save my team's time for the difficult cases, not the routine ones.
data.assistant_message: That makes sense. When you gather job opportunities from different places, what details do you keep about each job, and how long do you keep this information?

data.user_message: Well, the basics I'd want to know about any job — what the role is, where it's located, what skills or education the employer is looking for, whether it's full-time or part-time, and what the salary is if the employer is willing to share that. Location is actually really important to me because a young person in Hebron can't necessarily travel to Ramallah every day — so matching someone to a job they can physically get to matters a lot. As for keeping the information... like I said before, I'd want the data kept even after a job closes, because that's what feeds our statistics and reports. That historical data is actually really valuable for understanding what the labour market is doing over time.
data.assistant_message: I see, those details are crucial. Can you tell me more about how employers post job opportunities on your system and what specific information they need to provide?

data.user_message: This is really the heart of it for me. When a young person registers, I want to know more than just their name — I want to know their education, their skills, any work experience they have, where they're located, and honestly things like whether they have a disability, because we have a responsibility to make sure those young people aren't left behind. But the tracking piece — that's where I get genuinely frustrated. Right now a young woman might register at our Ramallah office and six months later nobody knows if she found work, if she ever got an interview, nothing. I want to be able to follow that journey — did she get matched to jobs, did she apply, did she get hired? Because without that I can't tell my minister or our international partners whether we're actually making a difference. That's the question I dread being asked because I have no real answer right now.
data.assistant_message: How do you currently keep track of people who sign up to use your job services, like if someone named Sarah registers to find a job, what information do you collect about her?

data.user_message: Right now it's painfully basic — my staff write things down on paper forms or maybe type something into a Word document or a simple spreadsheet. So for someone like Sarah, we'd probably note her name, maybe her phone number, her education level, and whatever certificates she brought with her. But there's no consistency — one office might record different things than another, and there's no central place where all of that lives. So if Sarah registered in Ramallah and then moved to Nablus, the Nablus office would have no idea she even exists. That's the kind of thing that makes me feel like we're genuinely failing these young people — the left hand doesn't know what the right hand is doing.
data.assistant_message: I see the challenge with consistency and centralization. When someone like Sarah signs up to find a job, what details do you keep about her, and how long do you keep this information?

data.user_message: Well beyond the basics like her name and contact details, I'd want to know her education — what level she reached, what certificates she has, where she studied. Her skills, even informal ones. Any work experience, even if it's just volunteering or short-term work. Where she lives, because geography matters for matching her to jobs she can actually reach. And like I mentioned, whether she has a disability, because we have programmes specifically for that. As for how long — honestly I'd want to keep her record as long as it's useful, even after she finds work, because that outcome data is exactly what feeds into our reports. If Sarah found a job in the construction sector six months after registering, that tells me something really important about what's working.
data.assistant_message: What should happen if Sarah's information changes, like if she gains new skills or moves to a different city?

data.user_message: She should be able to update that herself, I would think — I don't want my staff having to manually change everyone's details every time something shifts in their life. If Sarah completes a new training course or moves from Ramallah to Jenin, she should just be able to go in and update her own profile. Actually that raises something I care about — the platform needs to be simple enough that a young person with no technical background can use it without needing someone to hold their hand. Not everyone has a lot of experience with computers or smartphones, and I can't have a system that only works for the educated or the tech-savvy.
data.assistant_message: How do you ensure that the platform remains easy to use for everyone, including those with little technical experience?

data.user_message: Honestly, that feels a bit obvious to me — it just has to be simple, clear, straightforward. Big buttons, plain language, nothing complicated. But there's something more specific that I'd consider absolutely essential and I'm almost embarrassed it hasn't come up yet — it has to work in Arabic. That's the language my job seekers and most of my staff use every day. If it's only in English, or if the Arabic is poor quality, people simply won't use it. And I'd want it to work on a mobile phone too, because a lot of young people in Palestine don't have a laptop at home — their phone is how they access everything.
data.assistant_message: I understand the importance of language and mobile access. Can you tell me more about the User Account and Role Management aspects of your system?
